literal: thread, that which strings together; from √sīv 'to sew'
invariant_definition: An aphorism — the maximally compressed prose unit of foundational philosophical works. The classical definition (Vāyu-Purāṇa): *alpākṣaram asaṃdigdhaṃ sāra-vat viśvato-mukham | astobham anavadyaṃ ca sūtraṃ sūtra-vido viduḥ* (few-syllabled, unambiguous, essence-bearing, omni-directional, without filler, blameless — that is what the experts know as a *sūtra*). The *Brahma-Sūtra* of Bādarāyaṇa is the foundational *sūtra* of Vedānta. Vedānta is in part the long history of incompatible *bhāṣyas* on the same *sūtra*-text.

per_school.definition: The *Brahma-Sūtra* (also called *Vedānta-Sūtra*, *Śārīraka-Sūtra*, *Bhikṣu-Sūtra*, *Uttara-Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra*) is the school-defining *sūtra*. Each Vedāntic school's claim to legitimacy rests on its *bhāṣya* on this text.
primary_loci: Bhāṣyas: Śaṅkara, Bhāskara, Yādava-Prakāśa, Rāmānuja, Madhva, Nimbārka, Vallabha, Baladeva, etc.

translator_note: 'Aphorism' is acceptable but loses the thread-metaphor. The *sūtra*-genre is structurally sui generis — designed for memorization-with-commentary, not for stand-alone reading.
